coredata.dc:description: Unreasonable tunnel structure and low intrinsic conductivity limit practical applications of niobium oxide in high-performance supercapacitors. The study reports the construction of Nb9VO25 electrode material via coordination of Nb(V) and V(V) with histidine and serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSBGQD) and subsequent annealing. The introduction of HSBGQD and rambutan peel leads to formation of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystal and low valent Nb and V species. The combination of small size and more reasonable tunnel structure accelerates the ion diffusion. The Nb(IV) and V(IV) double doping optimizes the tunnel structure, narrows the bandgap and creates new pathways for high-speed electron transfer. The integration of defect engineering with graphene surface modification enhance the intrinsic conductivity. The Nb9VO25 electrode shows exceptionally high specific capacitance of 2925.3 F/g, which is more than 142 times that of Nb2O5. The symmetrical supercapacitor with Nb9VO25 electrodes and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte offers high specific capacitance (263 F/g at 1 A/g), high-rage capacity (138 F/g at 50 A/g), cycling stability (capacitance retention of 95.2 % after 10,000-cycle), energy density (146 W h Kg−1 at 996 W Kg−1 and 77 W h Kg−1 at 50181 W kg−1) and broad application prospect in wearable electronic devices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Unreasonable tunnel structure and low intrinsic conductivity limit practical applications of niobium oxide in high-performance supercapacitors. The study reports the construction of Nb9VO25 electrode material via coordination of Nb(V) and V(V) with histidine and serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSBGQD) and subsequent annealing. The introduction of HSBGQD and rambutan peel leads to formation of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystal and low valent Nb and V species. The combination of small size and more reasonable tunnel structure accelerates the ion diffusion. The Nb(IV) and V(IV) double doping optimizes the tunnel structure, narrows the bandgap and creates new pathways for high-speed electron transfer. The integration of defect engineering with graphene surface modification enhance the intrinsic conductivity. The Nb9VO25 electrode shows exceptionally high specific capacitance of 2925.3 F/g, which is more than 142 times that of Nb2O5. The symmetrical supercapacitor with Nb9VO25 electrodes and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte offers high specific capacitance (263 F/g at 1 A/g), high-rage capacity (138 F/g at 50 A/g), cycling stability (capacitance retention of 95.2 % after 10,000-cycle), energy density (146 W h Kg−1 at 996 W Kg−1 and 77 W h Kg−1 at 50181 W kg−1) and broad application prospect in wearable electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Following massive consumption of fossil fuel in the world, the use of solar energy, wind energy and tidal energy has been considered in large volume. To fully utilize these green energy sources, various energy storage devices were developed to overcome the adverse factors caused by the intermittency and far distance from towns of green energy sources [1–7]. Among various energy storage devices, supercapacitor received a more attention due to its high specific capacitance, power density and cycling stability [8]. However, the current supercapacitors only can offer a much lower energy density compared with electrochemical batteries. To enhance the energy density, many transition metal oxides have been investigated as pseudocapacitive electrode materials for supercapacitors [9]. Metal oxide often gives a higher specific capacitance compared with activated carbon, but its capacitance will rapidly decay with the increase of charging rate because of slow ion diffusion process and poor conductivity of metal oxide crystal [10]. Different from common metal oxide, sheer-structured Nb2O5 provides the interconnected tunnels in structure for the ion transport [11]. As a consequence, Nb2O5 has a much higher ion diffusion coefficient compared with common metal oxide. In addition, Nb2O5 can also store energy into the bulk of Nb2O5 nanocrystals via the intercalation/deintercalation of ion, producing the intercalation pseudo-capacitance [12]. Due to high ion diffusion coefficient, multiple redox and good structural stability, Nb2O5 become an ideal fast-charging electrode material for supercapacitors. Nevertheless, unreasonable tunnel structure and low intrinsic conductivity limit its practical applications in high-performance supercapacitors.

Several strategies were established for construction of Nb2O5 electrode material to improve the electrochemical property. The first strategy is to make the Nb2O5 nanocrystal with a special morphology such as nanowire [13], air filter [14] and microspheres [15]. An ideal morphology can provide the rich of channels for high-speed ion diffusion and increase the exposure of electroactive sites. Zhang et al. synthesized the Nb2O5 microsphere by hydrothermal treatment and subsequent calcination [15]. The Nb2O5 microsphere formed by resembling sweet dumplings with exposed fillings exhibits a high capacitance retention of 85 % after 5000-cycle for zinc-ion supercapacitor. However, the construction of Nb2O5 with complex morphology is difficult. It always requires multi-step reactions and precise control of reaction conditions. The second strategy is to make nano-sized Nb2O5 nanoparticles. The reduce in particle size brings more exposure of electroactive sites, leading to an increase in the specific capacitance. Lian et al. synthesized the Nb2O5 quantum dot via hydrothermal treatment, filtration, freeze-dry and thermal annealing [16]. Small size of several nanometers makes the nano-sized Nb2O5 nanoparticles offer a high specific capacitance of 189 mA h g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 for lithium-ion hybrid supercapacitor. However, the nano-sized Nb2O5 nanoparticle always gives a low tap density. This results in a low volumetric capacitance. The third strategy is to dope metal/non-metal ion into Nb2O5 nanocrystal [17]. The metal doping mainly has two purposes. One is to adjust the size of interconnected tunnels within the structure to achieve a high-speed ion diffusion. Another is to optimize the multiple redox system that can produce a higher pseudo-capacitance. The integration of above two factors will realize a high specific capacitance because of the increased diffusion redox capacity and high-rate capacity. To achieve the purposes, the multiple factors including atomic radius, valence electron structure and crystal structure need to be comprehensively considered. However, the research in this area is still in its early stage [18]. The fourth strategy is to introduce conductive carbon materials such as activated carbon [19], carbon nanotube [20] and graphene [21] to construct Nb2O5-based composites. Due to excellent conductivity, the introduction of conductive carbon material brings an improved electrochemical performance. However, great interface resistance between Nb2O5 nanocrystal and carbon material seriously suppresses the high-speed electron transfer between Nb2O5 and carbon coating. Actually, the effect of introducing carbon material on the electrochemical property is not ideal. This is because carbon material primarily enhances the electronic conductivity of environment surrounding Nb2O5 nanocrystals rather than its intrinsic conductivity. Up to now, scalable synthesis of an ideal Nb2O5 electrode material with excellent supercapacitor performance still faces great challenges.

In this study, we reported the construction of advanced Nb9VO25 electrode material (Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC) using histidine and serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSBGQD) and biomass carbon (BC). The introduction of HSBGQD leads to production of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystals, oxygen vacancies and graphene surface modification. The V doping optimizes the tunnel structure. The combination of HSBGQD and V doping achieves to high ion diffusion coefficient and high intrinsic conductivity. As a result, the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode material exhibits excellent supercapacitor behavior, including high specific capacitance, high-rate capacity, energy density and cycling stability.

Citric acid (CA), serine (Ser), histidine (His) and boric acid (H3BO3) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Ammonium metavanadate and niobium oxalate were purchased from Aladdin. The silver nanowires (Ag NW) in isopropanol (2 mg mL−1) was purchased from Jiangsu XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. The solid electrolyte (PVA/Li2SO4) was prepared by adding 2.0 g polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 10 mL of 2 mol/L Li2SO4 solution in 20 mL ultrapure water and then heated at 70℃ with stirring until a clear homogeneous solution was formed. Rambutan was purchased from Hainan Province and then rambutan peel was collected, followed by washing in distilled water and freeze-dried for use.

Morphology and structure were characterized by scanning electron microscope (SEM, JEOL, S-4800) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Jem-2100). The crystal structure and chemical composition were studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.15406 nm). Raman spectrum measurement was carried out using InVia laser micro-confocal Raman spectrometer (Renishaw, England). The valence states were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos, Axis supra) with mono chromated Al KR radiation. The band gap was evaluated by obtaining UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra by UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Shimadzu, UV-3600 Plus). The oxygen vacancies were characterized by electron paramagnetic resonance spectra (EPR, Bruker EMX PLUS, X-band≈9.8 GHz).

HSBGQD was prepared by heating the mixture of citric acid, histidine, serine and H3BO3 with a molar ratio of 1:1:0.2:0.2 at 180 °C for 3 h [22]. The resulted HSBGQD of 0.8 g was dissolved in 60 mL of ultrapure water. After added 0.15 g of ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3), 0.47 g of niobium oxalate (Nb(HC2O4)5), 10 mL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and 1 ml of ammonia (NH3), the reaction system was stirred until the system become a transparent solution. It was treated by sucking into 1 g of rambutan peel, drying at 60 °C for 8 h in vacuum and thermal annealing in N2 in sequence. During the annealing process, firstly raise the temperature to 400 °C at the rate of 1 °C min−1, keep the temperature for 1 h, then raise the temperature to 850 °C at the rate of 5 °C min−1, keep the temperature for 3 h, finally cool to room temperature naturally to obtain Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. The above procedure was also used for preparation of Nb2O5 unless no addition of NH4VO3, HSBGQD and rambutan peel, Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC unless no addition of NH4VO3, Nb9VO25 unless no addition of HSBGQD and rambutan peel, and HSBGQD-BC unless no addition of Nb(HC2O4)5 and NH4VO3.

The symmetrical supercapacitor with Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes and PVA/Li2SO4 gel electrolyte was constructed by 3D printing [23] (shown in Fig. s1). Firstly, silver nanowire (Ag NW) slurry was printed onto the surface of polyethylene terephthalate (PET) film, in which the feed printing temperature of 50 °C, line width of 0.1 mm and line thickness of 1.5 mm were adopted. Next, the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC ink was printed onto the surface of prepared current collector, in which the feed printing temperature of 75 °C, line width of 0.1 mm and line thickness of 0.1 mm were adopted. Next, the PVA/Li2SO4 gel was printed onto the surface of the prepared electrodes, in which the feed printing temperature of 80 °C and line thickness of 0.2 mm were adopted. The formed electrolyte larger covers the whole region between electrodes. Finally, the epoxy resin was printed onto the surface of soft supercapacitor to produce the symmetrical supercapacitor.

The mixture of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC, carbon black and polyvinylidene difluoride with a mass ratio of 90: 5: 5 were dispersed in N-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone under ultrasound and subsequently treated by ball milling for 24 h. The resulting Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC ink was used for the preparation of working electrodes in three-electrode testing system and symmetrical supercapacitor. One three-electrode testing system includes one titanium sheet working electrode bearing Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC (1 cm × 1 cm), one platinum foil counter electrode (1 cm × 1 cm) and one saturated calomel reference electrode. The aqueous electrolyte of 1.0 mol/L Li2SO4 was employed for three-electrode test system. For making one working electrode, the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC ink containing 1–5 mg of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC was coated onto the surface of one titanium sheet (1 cm × 1 cm). Followed by drying at 105 °C in vacuum for 24 h and finally pressed under 5 MPa for 1 h.

The electrochemical properties of both three-electrode system and symmetrical supercapacitor were measured by cyclic voltammogram (CV), chronoamperometry (CA), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and galvanostatic charge/discharge curve measurements on the CHI 660D electrochemical workstation. The potential amplitude of ± 5 mV and frequency of 0.01–105 Hz were adopted for the EIS measurement. For the three-electrode testing system, the specific capacitance of single electrode (Cg) can be calculated by the Eq. (1) [24]. Here, Cg, i, m, ΔV, and t present gravimetric capacitance (F g−1), current (A), active mass (g), potential range (V) and discharging time (s), respectively.

Fig. 1 presents the design and synthesis of HSBGQD and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. In the design of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC, HSBGQD plays important roles to improve the supercapacitor behavior of Nb9VO25 electrode. The first role is to convert Nb(V) and V(V) ions into the corresponding complex. HSBGQD contains many functional groups such as imidazole, hydroxyl and carboxyl, thus it can coordinate with Nb(V) and V(V) to produce water-soluble complex. The formation of Nb/V-HSBGQD complex inhibits the hydrolysis of Nb(V) and V(V). In addition, the coordination also slows down the release of Nb(V) and V(V) during thermal annealing. The above these factors result in the formation of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. The second role is to reduce Nb(V) and V(V) into low valent Nb and V species. Due to strong reducibility, HSBGQD and its derivatives from thermal decomposition partly reduce Nb(V) and V(V) to form low valent Nb and V species. The oxygen vacancy caused by Nb4+, V4+ and V3+ self-doping narrows the bandgap and generates the new pathways for high-speed electron transfer owing to the production of defect energy level. The third role is to modify graphene on the surface of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. During the thermal annealing, HSBGQD was reduced into small graphene sheets. The part of these graphene sheets were adsorbed on the surface of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals to produce the graphene surface modification. The integration of defect engineering with graphene surface modification significantly improves the electronic conductivity. Different from HSBGQD, the introduction of V(V) was mainly used for optimizing the tunnel structure in Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. The Nb2O5 nanocrystal offers one spacing of 3.94 Å for interconnected tunnels [25]. The spacing is obviously bigger than that of Li (H2O)6 + (2.21 Å) [26]. Such a large tunnels may cause one space waste and leads a reduced intercalation pseudo-capacitance. As Nb(V) offers a smaller radius than Nb(V), the introduction of V(V) reduces the size of interconnected tunnels and brings an increase in the intercalation pseudo-capacitance.

The as-designed Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC was synthesized by coordination of Nb(V) and V(V) with HSBGQD and subsequent thermal annealing. Firstly, Nb(V) and V(V) were converted into Nb/V-HSBGQD complexes. Due to excellent water-solubility of HSBGQD, the formed complex can dissolve in water to form one transparent yellow solution (shown in Fig. s2), which was rapidly sucked into rambutan peel (shown in Fig. s3). Then, the complexes were thermally annealed to the formation of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystals and three-dimensional carbon framework. The use of a high annealing temperature is beneficial to improve the crystallinity of Nb9VO25. However, high temperature annealing may bring serious agglomeration of Nb(V) and V(V) and leads to production of large Nb9VO25 nanoparticles. This is because a fast carbonization of rambutan peel and HSBGQD at high temperature causes a rapid escape of large amount of gas. This drives Nb9VO25 nanocrystals to move on the carbon framework and leads to the aggregation into large Nb9VO25 particles. To resolve the problem, one two-stage annealing scheme was adopted. The first stage uses a low temperature 400 °C with a slow heating rate of 1 °C min−1. In the stage, HSBGQD and rambutan peel were slowly carbonized to form three-dimensional carbon framework and graphene sheets. Due to a slow carbonization process, the produced gases will slowly escape from the system. This will effectively avoid serious agglomeration of Nb(V) and V(V) during the thermal annealing. The second stage uses a high temperature of 850 °C with a high heating rate of 5 °C min−1. In the stage, the formed Nb9VO25 precursor in the first stage was converted into Nb9VO25 nanocrystal. As the carbonization of rambutan peel and HSBGQD is close to complete in the first stage, a rapid escape of large amounts of gases cannot occur in the second stage.

Morphology and elemental composition of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC were characterized by SEM, TEM, STEM, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) and element mappings. Fig. 2 A shows the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC offers one three-dimensional structure composing of carbon framework and Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. The existence of three-dimensional carbon framework improves the ion/electron conductivity. The Nb9VO25 nanocrystal displays a small particle size with an average particle size of 21 ± 0.17 nm. There is one spacing of 0.357 nm on the HRTEM image, corresponding to the (121) facet of Nb9VO25 nanocrystal. There are many oxygen vacancies marked with yellow circles on the HRTEM image. These oxygen vacancies are produced by low valent Nb and V species self-doping. The result of element mapping analysis shows the space distribution of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), niobium (Nb), oxygen (O), vanadium (V) on Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Nb and V come from Nb9VO25, N come from HSBGQD, C come from HSBGQD and rambutan peel. Further, the element composition and Nb9VO25 content in the composite were measured by EDS and thermogravimetric analysis (see Fig. s4 and Table s1). The atom ratio of Nb, V and O is 2.61: 0.27: 7.09, which is equivalent to 9: 1: 25, and Nb9VO25 content is 21.74 % (wt.%).

Chemical environment of Nb9VO25-GQD-BC was characterized by XPS technique. Fig. 3 shows total XPS spectrum includes B1s, Nb3d, C1s, N1s, V2p and O1s six peaks, verifying the presence of B, Nb, C, N, V and O elements. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s could be divided into seven peaks at 282.12, 284.62, 285.66, 286.66, 288.11, 289.42 and 291.03 eV, corresponding to the C–B, C–C, C–O, C=O, O–C–O and π-π* bonds. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s could be divided into four peaks at 530.42, 532.56, 534.03 and 535.37 eV, corresponding to the C=O, C–O, O–C–O and O–C=O bonds. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of B1s could be divided three peaks at 190.46, 192.22 and 194.86 eV, corresponding to the B3C, BC2O and BCO2 bonds. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of V2p could be divided into six peaks at 514.59, 516.25, 517.02, 521.93, 523.44 and 524.75 eV, corresponding to the V3+2p3/2, V4+2p3/2, V5+2p3/2, V3+2p1/2, V4+2p1/2 and V5+2p1/2. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Nb3d could be divided into four peaks at 206.88, 207.76, 209.66 and 210.78 eV, corresponding to the Nb4+3d5/2, Nb5+3d5/2, Nb4+ 3d3/2 and Nb5+3d3/2. The above results also confirm the existence of low valent Nb4+, V4+ and V3+ on the surface of Nb9VO25 nanocrystal.

The crystal structure of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC were characterized by Raman spectrum, EPR spectra and XRD techniques. Fig. 4 A shows that there are seven weak peaks at the Raman shift of 100–1000 cm−1 and two strong peaks at Raman shift of 1000–2100 cm−1 on the Raman spectrum of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. The Raman peaks at 145, 286, 421, 522, 717, 885 and 998 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of V–O and Nb–O bonds. The Raman peaks at 1349 and 1608 cm−1 can be assigned at the D and G peaks of graphene. There are one pair symmetrical EPR signal at g = 2.002 on the EPR spectra of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC (Fig. 4B). An obvious difference appears in the EPR signal intensity of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. The EPR signal of Nb9VO25 is very weak, verifying that there are few low valent Nb and V species in Nb9VO25. Different from Nb9VO25, the EPR spectrum of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC offers one pair of strong EPR peaks, verifying the presence of more V3+/V4+/Nb4+ species. The presence of V3+/V4+/Nb4+ induces the production of rich oxygen vacancies in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC [27]. There are fourteen diffraction peaks at 22.71°, 23.69°, 26.85°, 29.19°, 32.73°, 35.14°, 38.38°, 40.34°, 46.75°, 53.08°, 55.41°, 60.76°, 62.79° and 67.62° on the XRD pattern of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC (Fig. 4C). One broad peak between 24° and 28° corresponds to the (002) plane of graphene. Due to poor crystallinity and low content of graphene in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC, the diffraction peak indicates weak and wide feature. The peaks at 22.71°, 23.69°, 26.85°, 29.19°, 32.73°, 35.14°, 38.38°, 40.34°, 46.75°, 53.08°, 55.41°, 60.76°, 62.79° and 67.62° correspond the (400), (101), (121), (510), (141), (600), (521), (710), (701), (471), (381), (622), (491) and (802) planes of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals, respectively. These diffraction peaks can well match with the different peaks in Nb9VO25-PDF# 083-9960. Thus, the result of XRD analysis demonstrates our synthesis solution achieves to the construction of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. Further, the enlarged XRD pattern reveals that the diffraction peaks of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC offers a reduced 2θ value compared with single Nb9VO25 (shown in Fig. s5). This is because the existence of oxygen vacancies in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC broadens the interlayer spacing of metal oxides [28]. Interestingly, all diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC exhibits a high intensity with a good symmetry. This shows the presence of oxygen vacancies cannot cause severe distortion and deformation of crystal planes in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC, verifying the oxygen vacancies are mainly distributed on the surface of Nb9VO25 nanocrystal [29].

The crystal structures of Nb9VO25 in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC at original, charge and discharge were shown in Fig. 4D and Fig. s6. Fig. 4D shows the Nb9VO25 phase consists of corner-sharing NbO6 octahedra forming 3 × 3 ×∞ ReO3-type blocks that connected to each other via VO4 tetrahedra in the same planes, indicating a typical bronze-like shear structure. The presence of interconnected tunnels in the structure accelerates Li+ ion diffusion in the structure. To confirm the suggest, the diameter (d) of interconnected tunnels of Nb9VO25 was calculated from Fig. 4D. The corresponding d value is 3.32 Å. The value is more than the ionic radius of Li(H2O)6 + (2.10 Å) [35], verify that tunnel structure in Nb9VO25 nanocrystal is sufficient to meet the needs of ion diffusion during the charging/discharging process. As the d value of Nb2O5 nanocrystal is 3.96 Å, the above result also demonstrates that the introduction of V(V) brings a reduced pipe diameter. Since the reduce in the diameter of tunnels brings an increases in number of tunnels per unit volume, the structural optimization caused by V doping achieves to a higher intercalation capacitance.

Fig. 4E and F present the plot of transformed Kubelka-Munk function vs. the energy of light and Motte-Schottky plots of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC, respectively. The results indicate that the bandgap energy (Eg) of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC is 0.68 eV, which is much lower than that of single Nb9VO25 (2.35 eV). The difference between Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC lies in the presence of rich oxygen vacancies in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Thus, the above result confirms the presence of oxygen vacancies narrows the bandgap energy, indicating a reduced Eg value. The Mott-Schottky plots of both Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC give one positive slope, verifying that both Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC are n-type semiconductors. The ECB values are −0.086 eV vs. Ag/AgCl for Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC (−0.51 eV vs. RHE) and −0.13 eV vs. Ag/AgCl for Nb9VO25 (−0.56 V vs. RHE). Based on the Eg and ECB, the valence band potentials (EVB) can be calculated by using the formula: EVB (V vs. RHE) = Eg + ECB. The obtained EVB values are 1.79 V for Nb9VO25 and 0.17 V for Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Based on the results of semiconductor properties, the energy band structures of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC and electron transfer mechanism were suggested at Fig. s7. Compared to Nb9VO25, Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC has a better structure of energy band. The Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC gives a narrower bandgap energy of 0.68 eV. Thus, the electron transfer only needs a low energy. This promotes the electron transfer within Nb9VO25 of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Secondly, the self-doping of V3+, V4+ and Nb4+ induces the production of defect level between the ECB and the EVB. This further narrows the Eg and leads to an improved electron transfer. Thirdly, graphene on the surface of Nb9VO25 nanocrystal acts as the electron trap. Such a surface modification will promote the electron transfer from Nb9VO25 nanocrystal to graphene. In general, an ideal structure of energy band makes Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC has a significant enhanced intrinsic conductivity.

Safe voltage window is crucial to an aqueous supercapacitor for avoiding the interference from H2 and O2 evolutions during charge–discharge process. Herein, CV and chronoamperometry techniques were used for detecting upper and lower limits of the safe voltage window of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode in 1.0 M Li2SO4 solution [30]. Fig. 5 A presents the CV curves of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode in 1.0 M Li2SO4 solution at different voltage ranges. No substantial leap can be seen in the cathodic current when the positive potential is lower than 0.8 V. However, the use of a more positive potential leads to a rapid increase in the cathodic current. Fig. 5B presents the chronoamperometry curves of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode in 1.0 M Li2SO4 solution at different voltage ranges. When the negative potential is lower than −1.2 V, the chronoamperometry curve displays a smooth feature. The corresponding current density is less than 0.1 A-1. Based on the results of CV and chronoamperometry investigations at different voltage ranges, a safe voltage window of 2 V from −1.2 to 0.8 V was obtained. Such a safe voltage window is wider than that of Nb9VO25 (1.8 V), Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC (1.8 V) and HSBGQD-BC electrodes (1.4 V). A wide safe voltage window for Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC should be attributed to the presence of V-doping and oxygen vacancies [31].

The charge/discharge curves and specific capacitances at current density of 1 A g−1 for HSBGQD-BC, Nb9VO25, Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes in 1 M Li2SO4 solution were shown in Fig. 5D and E. The discharge cure of C and Nb9VO25 electrodes are almost straight line. The HSBGQD-BC is an electrochemical inert material, it cannot be oxidized and reduced during the charge–discharge process. Thus, HSBGQD-BC can only produce EDLC. As a result, its discharge curve indicates a straight line feature. Nb9VO25 is an electrochemical active material, it can be oxidized and reduced by the transformation between different valent Nb and V species. Thus, Nb9VO25 can produce the pseudo-capacitance and EDLC. However, the contribution of pseudo-capacitance in the capacitance is very low owing to its extremely low electronic conductivity. Surprisingly, the specific capacitance of Nb9VO25 electrode (144F/g) is lower than that of HSBGQD-BC (216F/g). This is because the HSBGQD-BC has a much larger specific surface compared with Nb9VO25. Different from HSBGQD-BC and Nb9VO25 electrodes, the discharge curves of Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes include a rapid potential drop process in the range from 0.8 to 0.3 V and a slow potential drop process in the range from 0.2 to −1.2 V. The former produces EDLC and the latter will produce pseudo-capacitance. The specific capacitance of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is up to 805F/g, indicating a high specific capacitance. The value is obviously higher than that of Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC electrode (423F/g), verifying that the V-doping brings an increase of 382F/g in the specific capacitance. As the ionic radius of V(V) is smaller than that of Nb(V), its introduction optimizes the structure of interconnected tunnels within the nanocrystals. This brings an enhanced ion intercalation pseudo-capacitance. As a result, the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode shows a more specific capacitance compared with Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC electrode. The specific capacitance of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is more than five-fold that of Nb9VO25 electrode. This is attributed to the introduction of HSBGQD and rambutan peel during the synthesis. On the one hand, the use of HSBGQD effectively prevents the hydrolysis of Nb(V) and V(V) and finally leads to the formation of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. Small size increases the exposure of active sites on the Nb9VO25, leading to an increased capacitance. On the other hand, the use of HSBGQD and rambutan peel results in the reduction of Nb(V) and V(V) into Nb4+, V3+ and V4+ during the thermal annealing process. The self-doping of these low valent Nb and V induces the production of oxygen vacancies. The existence of oxygen vacancies narrows the bandgap energy of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. This will greatly improve the intrinsic conductivity and results in an improved pseudo-capacitance. In addition, the presence of conductive and three-dimensional carbon framework from rambutan peel offers excellent ion/electron conductivity. This will further improve the specific capacitance. Based on the mass ratio of Nb9VO25 and HSBGQD-BC in the composite, the specific capacitance of Nb9VO25 in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC composite can be calculated. The result shows the specific capacitance of W9NbO25 reaches 2925.3 F/g, showing a super-high specific capacitance. In addition, the result also demonstrates that our synthesis strategy fully unleashes the energy storage potential of Nb2O5.

The relationship curves of specific capacitances of HSBGQD-BC, Nb9VO25, Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes vs. the current densities were shown in Fig. 5F. For all electrodes, the increase of current density will cause a slow decrease in the specific capacitance. This is because the electrode reaction is slower than the charge–discharge process. Thus, the part of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC on the electrode surface has not yet undergone the redox for the charge/discharge. This leads to a reduced capacitance at a relatively big current density. However, four electrodes exhibit a different high-rate behavior. The specific capacitance of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is 805F/g at the current density of 1 A g−1. When the current density is up to 50 A g−1, the specific capacitance still remains 480F/g, indicating a good high-rate behavior. Different from the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC, the specific capacitance at the current density of 50 A g−1 will reduce to 40F/g of Nb9VO25 electrode, 70F/g of HSBGQD-BC electrode and 194F/g of Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC, showing a poor high-rate behavior. The above results demonstrate that Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC offers a much better high-rate behavior compared with HSBGQD-BC, Nb9VO25 and Nb2O5-HSBGQD-BC electrodes.

One symmetrical supercapacitor with Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes and PVA/Li2SO4 electrolyte was constructed to evaluate the supercapacitor performance of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Fig. 6 A and B present the CV curves of the supercapacitor at different scan rates. There are several pairs of redox peaks on the all CV curves. This is attributed to the oxidation and reduction of Nb and V species in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. With increasing the scan rate, the CV current will rapidly increase, verifying a good conductivity of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC.

The charge/discharge curves at different current densities and corresponding specific capacitances were shown in Fig. 6C and D. The discharge curve shows a slow potential decrease trend with the extension of discharge time. This demonstrates that the specific capacitance of as-proposed supercapacitor mainly come from the pseudo-capacitance caused by the oxidation and reduction of Nb-V species. As the electrode reaction is slower than charge–discharge process, the increase of current density will bring a slow reduce of specific capacitance. Thus, no enough time fully completes the oxidation and reduction of all Nb-V species in the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode. This causes a reduce in the specific capacitance. The specific capacitance reaches to 263F/g at the current density of 1 A g−1, showing a high capacitance. The value can keep 200F/g at the current density of 5 A g−1, 180F/g at the current density of 10 A g−1 and 138F/g at the current density of 50 A g−1. These data confirm the as-prepared supercapacitor has a good high-rate capacity.

The specific capacitances at the current density of 5 A g−1 for 10000-cycle were shown in Fig. 6E. At the first cycle, the capacitance of the first cycle is 200.0F/g. After 100000-cycle, the specific capacitance remains 190.3F/g, indicating a high retention rate of 95.2 %. The result demonstrates that the as-prepared supercapacitor offers an excellent cycling stability.

The relationship curve of energy density vs. the power density for symmetrical supercapacitor was shown in Fig. 6F. The energy density reaches to 146 W h kg−1 at the power density of 996 W kg−1, which is close to that of lithium ion batteries [32]. The energy density still keeps 100 W h kg−1 at the power density of 9944 W kg−1 and 77 W h kg−1 at the power density of 50181 W kg−1. The energy density is more than that of Co3V2O8-Ni3V2O8 [33], Ni3V2O8@CNTs [34], MnV2O6/G [35], BiVO4/GO [8] and WV2O7-G [36].

The use of wearable soft electronic devices is always accompanied by various deformation during the human movement. To test the possibility of as-prepared symmetrical supercapacitor in wearable electronic devices, several common deformations were designed. The corresponding specific capacitance change was investigated and the results were shown in Fig. 7 . With increasing folding/twisting angle, the specific capacitance will slightly reduce, indicating a good flexibility and electrochemical durability. Therefore, the as-prepared supercapacitor can be well applied in wearable electronic devices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy storage mechanism and dynamics behavior
ce:section.ce:para: The ex situ XRD patterns of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode were carried out at pristine, full charge and full discharge to prove the ion intercalation/deintercalation occurs in the charge–discharge process (see Fig. 8 ). At full charge, the XRD diffraction peak shifts towards high angle from 35.49° at the pristine to 35.79° at full charge. This demonstrates that the contraction of interplanar distance along the pristine angle direction occurs in the charging process. At full discharge, the XRD diffraction peak turns towards low diffraction angle from 35.79° at full charge to 35.48° at full discharge. This reveals an expansion of lattice spacing of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals occurs in the discharging process. The above phenomenon is because Li+ intercalation causes an increase of electrostatic attraction between the different layers in the Nb9VO25 nanocrystal. In addition, the phenomenon also is one key evidence for intercalation of Li+. More importantly, the Li+ intercalation results in the production of high intercalation pseudo-capacitance. On the one hand, an electron can be stored during a single Li+ intercalation. This makes the cation intercalation become more efficient. On the other hand, within Nb9VO25 nanocrystal there are many interconnected porous channels with large specific surface area and broad pore size distribution [15]. The features allow highly efficient Li+ migration into Nb9VO25 active sites and generates rapid oxidation and reduction of Nb-V species. Interestingly, the production of pseudo-capacitance via the intercalation of Li+ into the tunnels or layers of redox-active materials does not cause crystal deformation crystallographic phase transition. The characteristic is conductive to improve the cycling stability. In general, the result of XRD analysis demonstrates the presence of ion intercalation/deintercalation occurs in charge–discharge process of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode. This achieves to a high diffusion redox capacity.

The ex situ XPS spectra of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode were carried at pristine, full charge and full discharge to understand the energy storage mechanism (shown in Fig. 9 ). At full charge, the peak area fraction of Nb5+ increases to 30.82 % from 27.26 % at pristine. This confirms that the charging brings an increase of 3.56 % in the high valent Nb5+. However, the peak area fraction of Nb4+ decreases to 69.18 % at full charge from 72.74 % at pristine. The demonstrates that the charging leads to a reduce of 3.56 % in the low valent Nb4+. Similar to Nb, the charging results in an increase of 11.56 % in the high valent V5+, and decrease of 10.29 % in the low valent V4+ and 1.27 % in the low valent V3+. The above results demonstrate that Nb5+/4+ and V5+/4+/3+ pairs have high redox activity. A reversible process can be seen in the discharging process, showing the occurrence of solid-solution reaction and good reversible characteristic of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode. In addition, the V3d spectrum exhibits a more obvious valence change compared with Nb3d (shown in Table s2). This is because the redox potential of V is lower than that of Nb. In general, the results of XPS analysis demonstrates that Nb5+/Nb4+/V5+/V4+/V3+ are main electrochemical active species for production of pseudo-capacitance.

The ex situ EIS measurements were carried out at pristine, full charge and full discharge to evaluate the ion diffusion coefficient and electronic conductivity of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode. Fig. 10 presents the EIS curves of Nb9VO25 electrode and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode and corresponding linear relation between Z′ and ω-1/2 at low frequency region and the fitting data were shown in Table s3. The charge transfer resistance (Rct) of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC is 2.57 Ω, indicating a high electronic conductivity. The value is lower than 76.6-fold that of Nb9VO25 electrode, verifying that the introduction of HSBGQD significantly improves the electronic conductivity. Further, the diffusion coefficient (D) of Li+ was determined by the following Eqs. (3) and (4) [24]:

where R, T, A, n F, C, σ and ω is the gas constant (J mol−1 K−1), absolute temperature (K), electrode area (cm−2), electron transfer number, Faraday constant (C mol−1), Li+ concentration (mol L-1), Warburg diffusion coefficient and angular frequency (Hz). Firstly, the σ value of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes were obtained from the slope of the linear relation between Z′ and ω-1/2 at low frequency region in Fig. 10B. The σ value of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is 55.1, which was lower than that of Nb9VO25 electrode (70), indicating that the diffusion rate of electrolyte ions on Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is faster. Then, the obtained σ values were substituted into the equation (4) to calculate the D value. The diffusion coefficient of Li+ reaches for Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC is 9.66 × 10-13 cm2 s−1, which is close to 2 time that for Nb9VO25 electrode (5.98 × 10-13 cm2 s−1). In general, the results of ex situ EIS analysis demonstrate that the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC offers a faster ion diffusion process, higher electronic conductivity, and better electrode reaction dynamics behavior compared with Nb9VO25.

The CV behavior was investigated by varying the scan rate to evaluate the oxidation and reduction reaction dynamics of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Fig. 11 A and D present the CV curves of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes in a 1.0 M Li2SO4 solution at the scan rate of 5, 10, 20, 30 and 50 mV s−1. On the CV curve of Nb9VO25 there are two pairs of obvious redox peaks at −0.26/-0.35 V and 0.18/0.14 V. These peaks are attributed to the oxidation and reduction of V and Nb species on the electrode surface. Due to poor electronic conductivity, the CV curves of Nb9VO25 show a weak current density at the scan rates of 5, 10, 20, 30 and 50 mV s−1. Different from Nb9VO25 (1.8 V), Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC has a wider safe potential window (2.0 V). There are two pairs of an obvious redox peaks at −0.76/-0.45 V and 0.41/0.35 V on the CV curve of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Interestingly, the CV peak current for Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC is higher than 27-fold that for Nb9VO25, indicating an excellent oxidation and reduction reaction dynamics behavior. The increase on the CV current is due to high conductivity of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC. Further, relationship of the peak current (I, A) with scan rate (v, mV s−1) for Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC were investigated by the equation (5) [12]:
(5)
i = a
v
b

Based on the equation (5), the plots of LOG (I) with LOG(v) of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC were plotted in Fig. 11B and E, in which the slope of each plot presents the corresponding to b value. For the Nb9VO25, its b values are 0.50 for peak 1, 0.48 for the peak 2, 0.49 for peak 3 and 0.57 for peak 4. As b values of 0.5 and 1 presents the diffusion control and electrochemical reaction control, the charge storage of Nb9VO25 electrode is mainly controlled by diffusion control. Thus, the Nb9VO25 only can offer a low specific capacitance. Different from Nb9VO25, the b values for the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC reach to 0.63 for the peak 1, 0.65 for the peak 2, 0.64 for the peak 3, and 0.75 for the peaks 4. The data show that the charge storage is jointly controlled by the diffusion control and the electrochemical reaction control. With the increase of scan rate, the b values are gradually close to 1. The result demonstrates that at a high scan rate the charge storage of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC will mainly be controlled by the electrochemical reaction control.

The charge-storage mechanisms of Nb9VO25 electrode and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode were quantitatively explored by fitting CV curves at the different scan rates of 5–50 mV s−1 by Eq. (6) [37]:
(6)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v +
k 3
v
1 / 2

In the equation, k1, k2 and k3 reflect the contributions of electric double-layer capacitance from charge physical adsorption, electrochemical reaction capacitance from rapid surface redox reaction and diffusion redox capacity from redox reaction in the bulk electrode, respectively. The previous works have demonstrated that the specific capacitance of Nb9VO25-based electrode is composed of double-layer capacitance, diffusive redox capacitance and electrochemical reaction capacitance. Herein, the contributions of double-layer capacitance, diffusive redox capacitance and electrochemical reaction capacitance of Nb9VO25 electrode and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode at different scan rates can be calculated using Eq. (6). Fig. 11C and F indicate with the increase of scan rate the contribution rates of double-layer capacitance and electrochemical reaction capacitance increases. However, the diffusion redox capacity decreases with increasing the scan rate. This is due to an insufficient time for the diffusion of electrolyte ions into the interior of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC and subsequent redox reactions. An increase of scan rate from 5 to 50 mV s−1 causes a 15.6 % decrease in the contribution rate of diffusive redox capacitance for the Nb9VO25 electrode and a 28.3 % decrease for the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode. From the above data, it is easy to draw one conclusion: Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode provides a much slower diffusion of electrolyte ion into the interior of Nb9VO25 electrode compared with Nb9VO25 electrode. However, the conclusion is completely incorrect because the diffusive redox capacitance of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is too large than that of Nb9VO25 electrode.

To draw one accurate conclusion, the diffusive redox capacitances of Nb9VO25 electrode and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode at different scan rates were calculated by the CV curves in Fig. 11A and D and the results were shown in Fig. 12 . The most capacitances of Nb9VO25 and Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrodes come from the diffusive redox capacitance at all scan rates, thus enhance double layer capacitance becomes the key to improve the supercapacitor behavior of niobium oxide-based electrode materials. The diffusive redox capacity of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode reaches to 157.4 mA h g−1 at the scan rate of 5 mV s−1, which is more than 20 times that of Nb9VO25 electrode (7.8 mA h g−1). The value can remain 45.2 mA h g−1 at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1, which is more than 20.9 times that of Nb9VO25 electrode. In the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC composite, only Nb9VO25 can produce the diffusive redox capacity. Based on the mass ratio (21.74 %) of Nb9VO25 in Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC and the increase (20 times at least) in the diffusive redox capacity, we can conclude that: the diffusive redox reaction of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode is faster than 92-fold that of Nb9VO25 electrode.

For the purpose of comparison, the supercapacitor performances of all reported Nb and V-based electrode materials for supercapacitors were shown in Table 1 . Table 1 shows the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode offers the best supercapacitor performance among all reported electrode materials. Basing on the results of energy storage mechanism and dynamics investigations, we believe that superhigh supercapacitor performance of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode should be mainly attributed to four reasons in the following:

Firstly, the introduction of V element with a smaller atomic radius than Nb optimizes the structure of interconnected tunnels. This increases the number of tunnels and surface area within an unit crystal volume. The optimized tunnel structure leads to an increase in the diffusive charge capacity.

Secondly, the introduction of HSBGQD achieves to production of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystals, rich oxygen vacancies and graphene surface modification. Small size shortens the distance for the electrolyte ion/electron transfer during the charging and discharge process. The presence of oxygen vacancies narrows the bandgap energy and produces the special defect energy level. The graphene surface modification promotes the electron transfer from Nb9VO25 nanocrystals to graphene. The integration of defect engineering, small particle with graphene surface modification will significantly enhance the ion diffusion and electronic conductivity of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. This leads to an enhanced diffusive redox capacity and electrochemical reaction capacitance, especially high-rate capacity.

Thirdly, the introduction of three-dimensional carbon framework from rambutan peel improves the ion conductivity due to the existence of porous structures, electronic conductivity, structural stability due to an excellent mechanical property, and affinity of the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC with the polar electrolyte due to the existence of many hydrophilic groups, speeding up the electrode reactions. This leads to an improved high-rate capacity and cycling stability.

Fourthly, the combination of fast diffusion of electrolyte ion into the interior within the structure, high charge capacity and good electronic conductivity achieves to a superhigh diffusive redox capacity. The combination of good electronic conductivity of Nb9VO25 and high conductivity of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode achieves to superhigh electrochemical reaction capacitance. The combination of high structural stability of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC and good mechanical property of carbon framework achieves to a superhigh cycling stability.

The study demonstrated synthesis of high-performance Nb9VO25 electrode material by introducing histidine and serine-functionalized and boron-doped graphene quantum dot (HSBGQD) and biomass carbon (BC). The introduction of HSBGQD achieves to production of small Nb9VO25 nanocrystals, rich oxygen vacancies and graphene surface modification. The V-doping further optimizes the tunnel structure of Nb9VO25 nanocrystals. The formed three-dimensional carbon framework accelerates the ion/electron transfer during the electrode reactions. Since the integration of HSBGQD, V doping with BC brings a significant improvement in ion diffusion and electronic conductivity, the Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC electrode exhibits superhigh double-layer capacitance, electrochemical reaction capacitance and diffusive redox capacitance than common Nb2O5 electrode, especially diffusive redox capacitance. The Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC can be also used for lithium ion batteries, photocatalysis and biosensor. However, small particle size of Nb9VO25 leads to a relatively low tap density. This limits some applications of Nb9VO25-HSBGQD-BC in high-capacity energy storage devices.
Li Xiaoshan: Methodology, Investigation. Li Ruiyi: Methodology, Investigation. Li Zaijun: Methodology, Investigation. Yang Yongqiang: Investigation. Liu Xiaohao: Investigation.
